evidence_quotes.quote: However, it is common for publishers to close access to these games over time, despite customers having previously bought a licence for them.

paragraphs.text: Dear ladies and gentlemen, As a Belgian citizen, I would like to express my concern about an aspect that is not covered by the current Digital Fairness Act: the protection of consumer rights in the event of the disappearance of digital products, in particular video games. In the current digital landscape, more and more games are sold and operated exclusively online. However, it is common for publishers to close access to these games over time, despite customers having previously bought a licence for them. This phenomenon of deliberately disabling access to or removing a game seriously affects consumers’ rights. A product paid for disappears without any compensation. This is incompatible with fair trade. My personal concern goes beyond mere consumer protection. If we consider video games as cultural heritage and in many cases they are undoubtedly the deletion of these titles from the collective memory is comparable to book burning to make a profit. The destruction of these digital works, only to reduce maintenance costs or legal risks for publishers, leads to a structural loss of history and culture. Industry often claims that it is impossible or too expensive to keep older titles available. However, it is technically perfectly feasible to stop the ‘service’ component of a game, stop the sale and then make the game available free of charge as freeware. This removes the obligation to support, without the game being irreversibly disappearing. The problem is therefore not a technical inability, but a business choice to maintain control and block access. The Stop Killing Games campaign rightly notes that consumers increasingly pay for access that can be unilaterally withdrawn by the publisher. This undermines fundamental principles of property ownership and fair trade. Without clear legislation restricting this practice, we risk a future in which digital purchases are only temporarily leased, depending on the arbitrariness of the publisher. I therefore urge you to include explicit provisions when drafting and fine-tuning the Digital Fairness Act: Prevent publishers from terminating access to digital products without a valid reason; Give the consumer the right to continued access to paid digital goods; Enshrining by law the possibility of making discontinued games available as freeware, for conservation and public interest purposes. Video games are not merely consumer goods; they are an integral part of our digital and cultural heritage. Let’s make sure that legislation recognises and protects this. Yours faithfully, Thales Tomme
